speaker: interviewer
text: Interviewing Eva Shane. This is tape two. Eva, we left off speaking about your mother's journey from Antwerp back to Hamburg.

speaker: interviewee
text: To Hamburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you talk a little bit about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It turned out to be a nightmare. Her father had passed away. And as I mentioned, my mother was not Jewish originally. So her parents were, were not Jewish. They were Lutheran. And my-- that was in 1938. And my mother, of course, wanted to attend his funeral. And later she said, I wish I had-- somebody had warned me. I should have never done this.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she goes to Hamburg. And on her way, her brother meets her at the train station and tells her right away, you have to report to such and such-- to the Gestapo headquarters. They were here already and gave us a notice. You have to report right after you arrive. And she was devastated. She says, how could you not-- why didn't you warn me? How could you let me come knowing that the Gestapo? Because they apparently-- I don't know if they knew what the Gestapo meant, you know, but Gestapo was like-- almost like-- I mean, it was like a horror word, I would say.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so my mother went the same or the next day to the Gestapo. And she says it took hours there. They would insult her, yell at her, humiliate her non-stop.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like, at one point, he turns around-- this guy who was interviewing her-- turns around and he says, there was a file on the desk here a minute ago. Where did it go? And my mother said, did you-- in case you think maybe-- I, I didn't take your file. Here, you can-- you can check me. He says, well, with you people, you never know.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, and they would call her whore, and they would call all kinds of name, because she was married to a Jew. Absolutely-- and she said she did not expect to leave the building by herself. She did not expect it. And her brother, he must have suspected something also, because he took her there. He walked with her. And he said, I'm going to wait in front of the building. Just in case you don't come out, I know where you are. So they, they also knew. I don't know how many hours they treated her like this. And for some reason, they probably could not arrest her on the spot. See, they were always a little worried, because she came-- she had a different passport. She came from another country, so they were a little bit worried.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he-- and finally he let her go. And he said, when are you going to go back? She says like, three, three days later or whatever. What train? When are you leaving? What train? What station? And so she told them. He says, well, it's about-- because we can't wait to get rid of people like you. It's about time.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, just like, real insulting. And she said she goes on the train. And right on the border, Gestapo comes on the train-- in her compartment. So they must have-- how they knew where she was. Took her luggage, up-- inside out, threw everything out. They were looking for the tiniest little object so they would have an excuse to arrest her. They were still on German grounds. Tore-- took everything apart, her entire luggage. And they couldn't find anything, and then they left.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she was really close. She says, it was a miracle that I came back, that I made it back. Should've never gone in the first place. It was a nightmare. The whole thing was a nightmare, absolute nightmare.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she also says people had changed dramatically. Everybody was scared. Everybody was afraid. They would walk-- they were afraid to talk. Every time they would say a word that was not considered 100%, they would always look around to make sure nobody was following them, nobody was listening, nobody was spying.

speaker: interviewer
text: They-- everybody felt persecuted. But that's-- that's what the government wanted them to feel like, you know? They, they were holding them through fear to do-- to, to doing what they wanted them to do. Fear was a great weapon. And when your mother went back and she stayed with her family, these were not German Jews, were they, that she stayed with at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was her family. They were-- they were non-Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they still, you're saying, experienced--

speaker: interviewee
text: Her family was in danger too, because they came many times, you know, to their place and, and, and checking them out. And asking what happened, what-- how can you explain, you know, the fact that your daughter married a Jew and this sort of thing. And they got a lot of-- they got a lot of abuse and insults as a result, too, constantly. They, they also had to cope with a lot. And luckily, nobody got, got arrested from her immediate family. Luckily. But it was very-- and they made sure that life was going to be very unpleasant for them as a result, because they considered, you know, everybody marrying a Jew as some kind of a traitor or whatever. I don't know what they called them. It was a horrible thing in their eyes to do. Strangely enough, I don't know of many cases their-- where the wife divorced the husband or vice versa as a result. Even so, mixed marriages were quite common in Germany. I know quite a few families where they had mixed marriages, and I don't know of, of a divorce.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, 1938, your mother goes back. You're in Antwerp. And you told us about your childhood living there and, and some of the things you did for fun. Do you remember the first signs of, of danger or that things were changing?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very sudden. It was very sudden. I know the war started in September '39, but not for us. Our life was still going on normally. And people talked, and people got worried. And people got nervous, but life went on. And Belgium was not in war. And things were still normal. Until May 10, 1940, that was a few months later, when all of a sudden, I woke up one morning, early one morning, terrible noise, shooting. Nobody knew what was going on. And you saw these little clouds in the sky. They were shooting at planes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So here they were German planes coming and bombing the entire city-- without warning, without bothering to declare war. Nothing. They just came and bombed everything down. Just like they're-- in Rotterdam, they completely destroyed the entire city in one day, you know, completely. And people were not warned. They were not prepared.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they did the same with Antwerp. There was a lot of damage. And then right away, it was chaos, chaos. There was no school. Nothing was working. There were big signs. People had to report.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my father, who was still German-- he had his German passport-- had to report as enemy. You know, a lot of ironic things like this happened. And report was a blanket and some food. And he was deported. And we-- for months, we didn't know where he was. And they were mostly Jew, mostly German Jews. German and Austrian, that was the enemy, you know? And where did they have to report to?

speaker: interviewee
text: To some authority. I don't know. They had to gather some way. I forgot where it was. And then they put them on a train and took them off. And later, we found out they took him to a camp on the-- in Pyrenees. On the French, Spanish border there was a big camp. It was actually a nice, very nice area. And there were some people from the German consulate. Apparently, they didn't even know. Even the German consulate didn't know what was going to happen. So they were caught. And my father was kind of friendly with them. And they were there, too, together with Jews. [LAUGHS] With all the Jews. It was almost funny. All my father's friends were there, you know, all the Jewish-- the Jewish people he knew. And so they were in that camp for months, until one day they decided-- they were-- they were bribing one of the guards, and they were going out. You know, they had some, some people with money. And my father didn't have any money. So they-- so-- or they said, never mind. You'll go with us. They had a group of people. And so they had paid for, let's say, 10 people to go out one night. You know, these guards they did anything for money. You know, they didn't care.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they were counting-- one, two, three, four-- and then my father was the eleventh. And they say, hey, there's ten already. No, no, I'm the tenth. But they were gone already, so they couldn't ask them to come back. And they say, OK, go. So he sneaked out with the rest. And then it was almost a funny story because they didn't speak any French, most of these people. So how are they going-- how are they going to get along, you know? And there were-- there were-- everywhere they had these, these signs up with rewards for escaped prisoners, you know, to turn them in.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said usually somebody who, who escaped in the morning was brought back at night. They brought them back. [LAUGHS] It didn't, you know, it didn't take them very long, because they were dressed very shabby. And they didn't speak French, I mean, you know. So-- and then he was with this group.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were-- and so they didn't have-- you always needed papers. And you always knew. So how do you get a ticket, a train ticket, without papers? You couldn't. So they said what they did, they were-- they were waiting-- you learn all these tricks. They were waiting. And they were going to a station, and they were waiting when the train started to leave. See, they had the steam trains in these days. So they were not-- you know, the doors wouldn't close automatically and all that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they would say, oh, they would-- they would race like they were missing their train. They would race through the-- through the checkpoint, and-- so they would let them go. You know, they had to take the train and would just run on the-- on the going train already. The train had started. And they run in the compartment. And it-- and it worked. And they sit down. And here they were sitting across two gendarmes-- you know, policeman-- staring at them. [LAUGHS] But they didn't-- so they didn't speak, you know, during the whole train ride. They didn't speak, because they didn't speak French. They Didn't want to look suspicious. And they made it. You know, the gendarmes, they minded their own business. They were not the-- they didn't ask them any questions. And then my father would make it to Macon. Which was-- this, this is how he happened to be there, which is in southern France. And he decided not to come back to Belgium, which was smart, because Belgium was occupied. See, a few days later, or maybe a week later, Belgium was occupied by the German Army. You know, they were, they were all over. And so my father decided not to go back, rather stay there. And then he urged us to come and join him instead. And I remember him telling in his letters, leave everything, forget about the house. Forget about all our belongings. It's very dangerous. Come, come where I am. It's the only way we, we could manage to save our lives, because it was-- it was more lenient. It was not as strict. It was not occupied by Germany. They worked in collaboration with the German government, you know, Petain, as you may know. But it was not quite the same. Even so, they eventually-- they gathered all the Jews there too and deported them, but not right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Antwerp without your father? And what was that time like for you, and your mother, and your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, without my father, yes, it was very difficult. So after the bombings and everything was chaos. My father had disappeared. We didn't know where he was. We didn't know what's going to happen. Everybody was panicky, especially Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, they were running around, not knowing what are we going to do? What are we going to do? They knew the Germans are coming. We're trapped. We have to do something. And you did not really reason. You tried to run. That was the first instinct. You have to get away. You have to get away, one way or another. So there were trains, and they didn't charge you. There were-- there were trains and stations going to France. And so we decided we're going to go to France. I don't know why.

speaker: interviewee
text: Somehow that was the only opening, you know, the only gate that was open. We're going to go to France, and then we'll see. We'll find out-- oh, yes, we would try, I think, from France to go to England. Maybe there's a ship. We were trying-- I think the idea was to go to a French port and try to get a ship to England, to safety. How we were going to do this-- you know, you don't reason. You don't think.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we go to a station, and there were trains. And they were crammed with refugees. People were on the roof. They were hanging out of the windows. I mean, it was-- it was unbelievable. So we go on a train. And we go-- I don't know how far we got. So here we are-- my mother, my brother, and me. Of course, there was no food. There was nothing. So we sleep, you know, standing up. We sleep sitting, whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: Eventually, we were in France already. And eventually, it started getting pretty bad. There were battles. We heard cannons. We heard this. So the train was stopped, no more trains. Everybody had to leave. And I don't know exactly how, but we ended up being prisoners, made prisoners by British-- by, by a group of British soldiers and British officers. I don't know. They said they were going to-- going to protect us. But basically, they treated us like prisoners.

speaker: interviewee
text: So here we were a big group of women, mostly women and children. All Jewish, German and Austrian women and children, because we had German papers. So they-- we were there prisoners. We were enemies.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- but in a way, it worked out and kind of comical, because they put us all on a bus. And they said they're going to take us to, to England. They were going to take us to England. We say, fine. You know, that's where we wanted to go. Of course, they, they probably knew pretty well-- maybe that-- maybe they wanted to go to England, I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they took us here. There was an odyssey. You know, on this bus, there were a couple of British officers, high-ranking officers and soldiers with guns, with rifles, you know, protecting us. Against I don't know what.

speaker: interviewee
text: On this bus for several days-- and I remember, there was no food. There was no water. I remember them giving us water. Somebody-- one of the soldiers had found water, in a big container of water. And he says-- he came with a-- with a-- with a gun, pointing the gun at, at our chests. And he says, three gulps for each person. Everybody had to drink out of the same container, because there was no cups or anything. And they had-- they counted it, three gulps. I wonder if he had-- really would have killed us, you know, if [INAUDIBLE]. [LAUGHS] And there was this one old guy, there was one old guy. We, we called him grandpa. And he was absolutely obnoxious. Everybody hated him. And he, of course, he was grabbing this thing. He was drinking all he wanted. I guess everybody was hoping that the guy was going to shoot him, you know, to get rid of him. But-- [LAUGHS] And he was with two nieces who take-- took care of him. And they were Austrian. They were Viennese. And they were very pretty and very attractive and very sexy looking, these two girls with their grandfather. They actually took care of this-- of this horrible guy.

speaker: interviewee
text: And before you knew it, the officer went with one of them in the woods that night. I remember. I remember, I was thinking, what are they going to do out there, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And somebody tried to follow him. And he took his gun and he acted like he was going to shoot the person. [LAUGHS] They came-- they came-- they came back early the next morning with a cold. [LAUGHS] Anyways, that was part of my sex education I suppose. [LAUGHS] And-- OK. Then we stayed with, with these-- on the bus. And we ended up in Boulogne-sur-Mer. Boulogne is a port. And in a big, abandoned-- and the city, there was a town of Boulogne. And it looked like hell, like a nightmare. Corpses in the street, dead horses in the street. Dead-- everything was abandoned. So already that was a war. That was really the midst of war already. And they-- and they sheltered us. The odyssey ended in a big, abandoned train station.

speaker: interviewer
text: What country are you in now?

speaker: interviewee
text: France. France.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's in France?

speaker: interviewee
text: North-- Northern France.

speaker: interviewer
text: That city you mentioned--

speaker: interviewee
text: Boulogne-sur-Mer, that's Boulogne on the-- Boulogne on the sea. That's on the North Sea. It's a port on the North Sea. But the thing was abandoned. I don't know where the population was. When I think back, they must have fled. You know, they probably heard that the Germans were coming, and they fled. I don't know where.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year--

speaker: interviewee
text: But the streets were like empty.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year are we in still?

speaker: interviewer
text: And you are how?

speaker: interviewee
text: In May, 1940, I was 12. And so they said-- so the-- and they were soldiers, British and French soldiers defending this train station, which supposedly was the last, their last fort or whatever where they--

speaker: interviewee
text: So they broke-- I remember, with their rifles-- they broke the windows all around, and then standing behind those windows and shooting. And then the shooting started like crazy. So they came with some sandbags. So they felt, you know, they had to do something for us. The came with sandbags and said, here. They put sandbags up for protection for us. So we were hiding in this little corner there, surrounded by sandbags. And I remember there was this one-- some of-- some of the people-- it is interesting, when you're in a situation like this and you think this is the end, how people react differently. Some people stay very calm, you know? And, OK, so I'm going to die. Big deal. And some people, they go completely crazy. They, they have these, these nervous breakdowns. There was this guy, he was screaming and crying and, ah, you know? God save me and all that. And I was looking at this guy. I said, this guy it crazy, you know? I-- it didn't bother me that much. I learned to-- and I did that later too-- to detach myself in a way from reality, you know? Just like looking at myself from, from, from a distance. Hey, what are you doing here, you know? And I thought, I'm going to die. So what? It's no big deal. This sort of attitude.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's the only thing that can-- that helps you survive in a horrible situation. But not everybody has this ability. I heard other people say that too. And I realized-- And another thing that other people say, you can never, the last thing you should do, have a breakdown or start crying and feel sorry for yourself. You can never-- you never have that luxury of doing this. The minute you feel sorry for yourself, you breakdown, you're gone. In concentration camps, they said that too, you know, survivor from concentration camps. Don't cry. You knew that. Whoever broke down was a goner. You know, you always had to be above the situation, no matter what happens. We would make jokes. You know, we had this little group-- we had-- there was a boy our age, my brother and me, and we would-- we would crack jokes and things, you know, to survive. You escape reality somehow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like later, I started-- I, I would write comedies instead of tragedies, you know? I would write-- we would write comedies. I still have--

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] I still have one. But you, you know, you can't let yourself go. And, and you know, it's--

speaker: interviewer
text: In that-- where you were at that time then, what, what happened afterwards with that situation?

speaker: interviewee
text: So then there was this big battle. And I watched like, like guys-- you know, corpses. You had to walk over corpses. I saw corpses cutting into hundred pieces, pieces of meat. And they try to find somebody's identity. They had to go through all that meat and blood and find if they had some kind of identification.

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw a French soldier standing right next to me, and there was a shot through the-- through a window, right in his eye. And I still see blood stream coming out of his eyes, this-- coming down, you know? This big-- light-- and I was looking at this and said that's strange. You know, it's such a light color of blood. It was very light color of blood.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he took his helmet-- shows how you don't know what you're doing-- he took his helmet to catch the blood that was coming out. You know, for-- senseless, completely senseless.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is fighting who? The British--

speaker: interviewee
text: The British and French were fighting the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans had surrounded this this-- a station, completely surrounded.

speaker: interviewer
text: The port?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were surrounded by the-- by the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm. And they tried to fight. I don't know why. You know, it was senseless, completely senseless. And at one point, an officer came to us. And he says, take your luggage and follow me. And I figured he was going to bring us to safety or somewhere. So he took us out. And he says, go down, your head down, heads down, heads down. And there was shooting, shooting, shooting. And you could hear the bullets, you know, fly over your heads, and your head. And some people-- we saw people fall. And we saw people fall. They're probably dead or wounded, whatever. And then that's-- that's-- nobody cared, you know? If you fall down. You also become desensitized. You see corpses, and it doesn't mean anything anymore. And they took us all around the station, you know, this group of refugees. And then back and back where we were. And we look at each other, what is that for? Later we thought they just wanted to show the Germans that they were civilians, as if the-- as if it would make a difference to them really. You know, they were so sensitive, you know? They-- sure. For no reason at all.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then finally somebody came. And he says, OK. And he took us, and, and we went down to the cellar, to the underground. There was an underground. It was all dark. And, and so we were relatively safe. And I don't know how long we stayed in there, because you couldn't tell the difference between night and day. And of course, there was no food, no water, whatever. We would just stand there. There was not even room to sit. And then after so many hours, or days, or I don't know what, suddenly a door upstairs opened. And somebody says, OK. You can come out. And the first thing we see was this German, was in in full uniform, with rifles, with his helmet and everything. And he says, OK, OK-- in German-- OK, come out. Come out. Come out. And we all come out, and we look at this guy. And we didn't quite understand what was happening. And then we see all these-- and then all the British and the French, they were all prisoners. They were all lined up. And they looked-- it was pitiful. They hardly actually had any equipment. They were still using guns from World War I and some uniform, old equipment. I mean, and then you say you saw the Germans. They were equipped something unbelievable. Like they had been preparing for war for years and years and years. And the Allies, they completely ignored that. They were taken by surprise. I mean, that's something up to this day, I can't understand. But anyways, here where all the Germans. And they were the masters. I mean, you know, right away you could tell they were the masters. And the others were the prisoners.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother comes up to him, and said, OK, that's it. We're prisoners now. And she asked him, where do you want us to go? And he says, go anywhere except there. There, they're still fighting. So then we knew we could do whatever we wanted. You know, we were free. Couldn't believe it. And so then we decided what are we going to do? We have no place to go. We have to try to go back home now. You know, start all over. If we had lost all our luggage. We had taken a lot of valuable stuff, like silver, that we figured, you know, we'd need. We're going to sell. And-- but the whole train was bursting up in flames at the end. Everything was gone, or maybe people came to steal it. Who knows? So our luggage was all gone. And so we figured, how are we going to go-- so how are we going to go back to, to Antwerp? We ended up with a group of other Jewish refugees in a-- on a truck, on a German truck, in a-- in a-- in a German army column.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they took care of us. They gave us food, everything. We sat at the back of a truck, you know, with-- on a military truck.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] And that's how you got back to Antwerp?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we got back to Antwerp. It took like a few days, yeah. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So what year are we? Still 1940?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. 1940. So we went all the way back to Belgium. I mean, that was the only-- those were the only-- the only transportation on the roads, you know? There was no, no cars, no private-- no nothing else, because they took over the roads. And they took us. They didn't know we were Jewish, of course. You know, we didn't tell them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have papers with you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't check the papers. You know, they didn't care. But they, they were still in battle. They were going from one battle to another, so they-- they had other problems-- [CLEARS THROAT] --on their mind. And I remember, one night-- they would stop at night. Of course, it took several days. I don't know exactly how long. One night-- and, and there were lots of women. They're all Jewish, women and children with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they never asked us any questions. They were just happy that we spoke German. You know, that made them happy. They didn't ask us, where do you come from? How do you happen to be here? Nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: We stopped in a village, in a French village. And so they said, OK. Tonight we're going to go in a motel-- hotel, whatever, they call it, you know. It's a big name for a little thing with-- they, they had some beds. And we walk in there. And he says, OK. Tonight we're going to make sure you're going to sleep in a bed and, and so on. And we say, oh, wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we walk in there. And there was this, this like-- like a little restaurant, you know? French restaurant or whatever. And there was this big group of SS. They were sitting there celebrate, singing their songs. And they were drunk, most of them. Oh, come here. And here-- you know, women, and speak German. Yay, women. German women and whatever. You know, they got all excited. And I thought, oh, god. What is this? And so they, they-- come on, sit here. You know, Have a beer or whatever. And you know, what can you do? You don't say no to an SS. So we sit down.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was-- I was by myself. I was-- I was separated from my mother and, and, and, and, and-- next thing I remember, I was sitting at a table. I was surrounded by SS. One guy he had-- this-- his arm around-- I was 12 years old. He has his arm around me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I see his, blood-shot eyes. And they were singing songs. And, and he grabbed me. And I thought, oh, god, what am I going to do? And my mother and this other woman who was a friend of her, they were being, being chased by some other SS guy.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] And all of a sudden, there was this big-- this officer comes in. And he looks at her. He says-- and they all stand up to attention. As drunk as they were, you know, they still had discipline. They stand to attention.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says, everybody, everybody up-- everybody go upstairs. And they disappear. And in one second, they were gone. And he apologized to us. And he says, yeah. I'm sorry that this happened. And they were punished. I know, I know, he got them because they were out of-- luckily, I mean, he saved us. Who knows what would have happened? And this is your journey back-- on your way back to Antwerp?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the journey back to Antwerp. Yeah. And then, we finally made it back to Antwerp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how long-- how far you were from Antwerp? Is this days?

speaker: interviewee
text: A few days, it was a few days. Strangely enough, that was in May 1940. And it was unusual good weather that, that whole month, because there were millions of refugees all over the area. And people had to sleep outdoors. And, you know, practically live outdoors during that time. And so that was a great help.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at this time, you still have not heard from your father? There is still no word?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We didn't know where he was. We didn't know where he was. We didn't know if he was alive or not, and where he was. We had no idea. You know, no communication whatsoever. Much, much later, we started hearing from him.
